title: Natural Language Processing (NLP) based verbal fluency markers as differential diagnostic indicators of early psychotic, affective and clinical high-risk states
description:
Background: 
Language disturbances in psychotic and affective disorders persist beyond general cognitive dysfunction, yet the diagnostic value of computationally derived linguistic features relative to standard cognitive assessment remains unclear.
Aims: 
To evaluate whether features derived using natural language processing (NLP) methods from a brief verbal fluency task (VFT) allow classification of early-stage psychotic and affective disorders or if language-based classifiers demonstrate transdiagnostic generalizability.
Methods: 
Forty individuals with recent-onset psychosis (ROP), 50 with recent-onset depression (ROD), 33 at clinical high-risk for psychosis (CHR), and 54 healthy controls (HC) from the PRONIA study were investigated. Sixty-two NLP features were extracted from verbal fluency tasks. Five elastic net classifiers were evaluated using nested cross-validation, with cross-group transfer and staging analyses assessing severity across the four groups.
Results: 
NLP-only models achieved classification accuracy comparable to a four-domain cognitive battery (MCCB) across all comparisons (pFDR > 0.54). Classifiers trained on one diagnostic comparison generalized to others. Staging analysis confirmed the predicted dimensional ordering HC < ROD = CHR < ROP. Group differences were driven by verbal productivity and search organization rather than semantic coherence.
Conclusions: 
A two-minute verbal fluency assessment with automated NLP analysis matches a comprehensive cognitive battery for diagnostic classification. Linguistic features capture a differential-diagnostic marker that generalizes across diagnostic boundaries and reflects a dimensional severity gradient, supporting their integration into clinical screening or monitoring for early psychotic and affective disorders.
authors: Millenia Chakraborty, Julian Wenzel, Louisa Schwed, Felix Menne, Alexandra König, Annkathrin Böke, Hannah Hacker, Carolina Bonivento, Alexandra Stainton, Dominic Dwyer

title: The Poker Face Tax: Coherence Between Facial Expression and Subjective Experience as a Predictor of Depression Outcomes in Psychological Treatment
description: Emotion coherence, the within-person alignment between subjective emotional experience and outward facial expression, is theorized to serve social-communicative functions, particularly in help-seeking contexts. Despite its theoretical importance, no study has directly tested whether within-session emotion coherence predicts depressive outcomes in psychotherapy. Using multilevel Response Surface Analysis (ML-RSA) in a sample of 65 patients (205 sessions) with major depressive disorder undergoing psychotherapy, we examined whether session-level congruence between self-reported mood and automated facial expression coding predicted subsequent depression scores and whether expressive suppression moderated this effect. Results indicated that greater within-session coherence predicted lower subsequent depressive symptoms, whereas increasing discrepancies between the two were associated with worse outcomes (curvature along the line of incongruence, a₄). Importantly, this effect was directional: sessions characterized by “feeling more than showing”, that is, higher self-reported than facially expressed negative affect, were particularly predictive of worsening depression (slope along the line of incongruence, a₃). These coherence and asymmetry effects were moderated by expressive suppression. Among high suppressors, the link between coherence and lower subsequent depression, as well as the cost of “feeling more than showing”, remained pronounced. In addition, coherence itself became more informative, with sessions marked by jointly elevated reported and expressed negative affect predicted lower subsequent depression, whereas jointly low affect predicted higher subsequent depression (slope along the line of congruence, a₁). These findings carry important theoretical and clinical implications, establishing emotional coherence as a core process in help-seeking environments and a potential therapeutic target, particularly for individuals high in expressive suppression.
authors: Hadar Fisher, Avigail Bar-Sella, Yael Bouknik, Dennis Kivlighan, sigal zilcha mano

title: Development and validation of a Japanese version of the Sensory Experience Questionnaire (SEQ) 3.0: Structural differences of sensory features across autistic children
description: People with autism spectrum disorder (ASD) often have unique sensory experiences. For both research and clinical purposes, tools that measure sensory features in autism are valuable; however, a gold-standard tool does not yet exist. The Sensory Experience Questionnaire (SEQ) 3.0 is a well-established caregiver-/parent-report questionnaire for autistic children and other neurodivergent individuals. This study aimed to develop and psychometrically validate the Japanese version of the SEQ 3.0 (SEQ-J). After validation, we examined the differences in sensory response patterns among children with ASD, those with other neurodevelopmental conditions, and typically developing (TD) children to determine whether the SEQ assesses ASD-specific sensory features. We recruited 154 children with ASD (44 with attention-deficit hyperactivity disorder [ADHD] and 66 with other conditions) and 156 TD children, aged 6 to 12 years. Confirmatory factor analyses revealed an acceptable model fit for four content factors (sensory hyper- and hyporesponsiveness, sensory interests, repetitive and seeking behaviors, and enhanced perception) and six method factors (five sensory modalities and social context) in both children with and without ASD. Receiver operator characteristic (ROC) analyses showed that SEQ factors, except for enhanced perception, demonstrated good classification performance between ASD and TD groups. However, ROC analyses comparing children with ASD to those with comorbid neurodevelopmental conditions did not show satisfactory performance. We performed representational similarity analyses (RSAs) on all ten SEQ factors, comparing both between- and within-group data. The RSAs revealed no difference in within-group similarities between the ASD group with no other developmental conditions and ASD + ADHD groups, but a significant difference in the overall configuration of scores between diagnostic groups compared to other group combinations. These findings support the psychometric validity of the SEQ-J for measuring sensory response patterns specific to children with ASD. Additionally, the structural configuration of SEQ subscores may be useful for investigating sensory features across diagnostic conditions, including ASD.
authors: Takeshi Atsumi, Haruka Ito, Akane Shinjo, Tomoe Fukutomi, Yasuo Terao, Masakazu Ide, Kanae Matsushima

title: Item Response Theory Development of a Brief Parenting Measure for Diverse Families: The MAPS V2 Parent-Report
description:
Abstract
Parenting is a strong predictor of child and caregiver mental health, yet many parenting measures are burdensome or perform unevenly across diverse families. This study developed and validated an ultra-brief parent-report measure derived from the Revised Multidimensional Assessment of Parenting Scale (MAPS V2). Using a national sample of parents of children ages 3–17 (Sample 1; N = 1,699), items were selected with item response theory graded response models to maximize information while minimizing length. The resulting 10-item MAPS V2 Brief Parent-Report assesses Positive Parenting, Hostility, and Lax Control. Confirmatory factor analyses supported a three-factor structure with excellent fit (CFI = .998, TLI = .997, RMSEA = .029, SRMR = .029) and acceptable to good reliability (ω = .75–.81). Criterion-related validity was supported by expected associations with caregiver depression (PHQ-9), anxiety (GAD-7), and child difficulties (SDQ), with Hostility and Lax Control showing stronger links to psychopathology than Positive Parenting. The factor structure and reliability replicated in an independent clinical sample (N = 110), demonstrating excellent fit and comparable reliability (ω = .74–.83). These findings support the MAPS V2 Brief Parent-Report as a psychometrically sound, low-burden measure for research and applied settings.
authors: Violeta Rodriguez, Mandy Witkin, Jennifer Hellmuth, Justin Parent

title: Effects of Process- and Embodiment-Focused Psychology (PEP) Treatment for Depression, comorbid Anxiety and Generalized Self-Efficacy: A Quasi-Experimental Pilot Study
description:
Background
Depressive and anxiety disorders are highly prevalent and frequently co-occur, highlighting the need for accessible and
low-threshold treatment approaches. Process- and Embodiment-Focused Psychology (PEP) is an integrative intervention
combining cognitive techniques with embodiment-focused procedures such as sensorimotor self-stimulation and
structured work with self-referential cognitions. To date, however, no controlled studies have systematically examined
the effects of therapist-guided PEP treatment on depressive symptoms, anxiety symptoms, and generalized self-efficacy.
The present pilot study therefore investigated whether PEP treatment is associated with changes in these outcomes
compared to a literature-based information condition.

Methods
Twenty-five adults (12 experimental group, 13 control group; age range 18–58 years, M = 43.48, SD = 11.51) with
depressive symptoms and in part with comorbid anxiety symptoms (n = 15), participated in a quasi-experimental
longitudinal study with two assessment time points. Participants received either therapist-guided PEP treatment (EG) or
engaged in structured self-study using a PEP-based self-help manual (CG) over approximately six weeks. Depressive
symptoms (BDI-II), anxiety symptoms (BAI), and generalized self-efficacy (GSWE) were assessed at baseline and post-
intervention. Mixed-design repeated-measures ANOVAs were conducted to examine time × group interaction effects.

Results
Significant improvements over time were observed for depressive symptoms, F(1,23) = 121.15, p < .001, η²p = .84,
anxiety symptoms, F(1,13) = 89.08, p < .001, η²p = .87, and generalized self-efficacy, F(1,23) = 41.97, p < .001, η²p = .65.
Significant time × group interaction effects indicated stronger symptom reductions and larger increases in generalized
self-efficacy in the therapist-guided PEP condition (BDI-II: F(1,23) = 27.23, p < .001, η²p = .54; BAI: F(1,13) = 323.00, p
< .001, η²p = .96; GSWE: F(1,23) = 5.51, p = .028, η²p = .19). Within-group effect sizes were large in the experimental
group (BDI-II: d = 3.05; BAI: d = 2.31; GSWE: d = 1.93). In the control group, within-group effect sizes were also large for
depressive and anxiety symptoms and moderate-to-large for generalized self-efficacy (BDI-II: d = 1.24; BAI: d = 1.24;
GSWE: d = 0.77).

Conclusions
The findings provide preliminary evidence that therapist-guided PEP treatment may contribute to reductions in
depressive and anxiety symptoms and to increases in generalized self-efficacy. Although causal conclusions are limited
due to the quasi-experimental design, baseline group differences, and small sample size, the results support the
potential clinical relevance of PEP as an integrative and resource-oriented intervention approach. Further randomized
controlled studies are needed to confirm these findings.
authors: Laura Grabener, Can Kulens, Anne Martinelli

title: Peer support in employment services for people living with psychotic disorders
description:
Background
Psychotic disorders are associated with significant barriers to employment. Even if work can support recovery and improve social functioning, most people living with psychotic disorders remain unemployed. Supported employment programs help these individuals integrate the job market, but we observe a lack of documentation about peer support interventions in those services. This paper presents the protocol for a study aimed at exploring the underlying factors through which peer support may influence employment among people living with psychotic disorders within the socio-cultural and economic context of the province of Quebec, Canada. 
Methods
This qualitative research was developed in collaboration with a specialized community organization using a participatory approach. Workshops were led by peer workers, addressing different themes such as coping with illness, disclosure, and social skills. Data is still collected through focus groups with three categories of stakeholders: (1) participants, (2) peer workers and (3) employment counselors. Data will be analyzed using Braun and Clarke’s inductive thematic analysis, supported by NVivo, with member checking and reflective journaling to strengthen rigor.
Discussion
This study is expected to contribute to an emerging area of research by documenting how peer support intervention may help promote recovery by improving employability for people living with psychotic disorders. Results are expected to highlight how lived experience, as an expertise, can play a role and complement employment services when it comes to accessing the job market as a person living with psychotic disorders. This research could help develop a more inclusive, community-based, and scalable employment programs and services. 
Keywords
Peer support, severe mental illness, psychotic disorders, employability, work
authors: Salma Bachir Cherif, Genevieve Sauve, Amal Abdel-Baki, Srividya Iyer, Chad Chouinard, Simon Longpré, Tania Leduc

title: Understanding and Leveraging Men’s Affinity Toward AI-Based Mental Health Support
description:
Men consistently underutilize mental health (MH) support despite substantial psychological burden. Traditional masculinity ideologies, gender role strain, and the precarious nature of masculine identity have been identified as central barriers to help-seeking. At the same time, general-purpose large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used for MH-related sup-port, often outside formal care pathways. 

This theoretical manuscript integrates the Gender Role Strain Paradigm, Precarious Man-hood Theory, an adaptation of the Health Belief Model, and the Unified Theory of Ac-ceptance and Use of Technology (UTAUT) to explain why LLMs may lower masculinity-related barriers to MH. As UTAUT validation studies in MH frequently fail to identify gen-der-effects, we conclude that their generalization is limited and that actual utilization rates should be regarded. Furthermore, we argue that LLMs uniquely combine several facilitating conditions and can become a cue to action themselves. While a gendered treatment gap per-sists despite promises of established eMH, utilization of LLMs is gender-balanced as users can ask questions within a familiar, non-clinical tool. 

The resulting high off-label use calls for safety guardrails and regulation, and we suggest a bidirectional strategy: effective bridge mechanisms to established MH need to be identified, facilitating a gender-sensitive gateway into MH. Second, depending on their current and foreseeable capability, general-purpose LLMs should be classified as a restricted medical product. We conclude by formulating three gender-related hypotheses and a Reconstruction-Deconstruction Model, to describe the current state of research, and to reduce gendered entry barriers while responsibly guiding men beyond masculinity-congruent engagement.

title: A group-based prevention program fostering mental health and physical activity: Pre- to post-program outcomes and session-level measures
description:
Objective: Physical activity (PA) and psychoeducational sessions (PsycEd) represent popular and effective components of well-being-oriented prevention programs. However, while program-level (distal) improvements are well-documented, less is known about session-level (proximal) processes and their relation to overall program effects. 
Methods: We investigated an 8-week, naturalistic, non-controlled, health-insurance-provided group-based prevention program comprising weekly outdoor PA and PsycEd in individuals often experiencing low mood, energy levels, tiredness, or irritability. As proximal outcomes, 80 participants reported their current mood and stress (time pressure and burden) before and after session times. They further rated each session on social support, group atmosphere, and helpfulness. Engagement was operationalized as session attendance. Changes in affective well-being (WHO-5) were assessed as the program-level, distal effect. Its moderation by session-level measures were analysed.  
Results: WHO-5 scores improved significantly from pre- to post-program (d=.25). Significant proximal improvements in mood (d>.60) and stress burden (d>.20) were found for PsyEd and PA. Only PA sessions were significantly linked with time pressure reductions (d=.17), while those sessions were also rated higher on social support (d=.65), group atmosphere (d=.49), and helpfulness (d=0.32). There was no significant difference between session types in attendance. Participants attending more sessions had higher WHO-5 improvements (β=.18), but no other session-level variable significantly moderated this improvement. 
Conclusion: While the study’s limitations of the missing control group, the potential mismatch between distal and proximal measures, and the mostly female sample should be considered, potential program effectiveness might be boosted by targeting continued attendance. Outdoor PA might be slightly superior to PsycEd in activating group benefits and could potentially be expanded.
authors: Mareike Röttger, Jens Blechert, Matthias Aulbach

title: Men’s Experiences of Mental Health Services for Depression: Perceptions, Expectations, and Satisfaction
description: Men’s reluctance to seek help for depression persists, with low engagement and high dropout rates in mental health services despite efforts to reduce barriers. Understanding men’s help-seeking journeys is therefore important for improving engagement and access to support. This qualitative study explored men's perceptions, expectations, and satisfaction of mental health support for depression, focusing on how these dimensions interacted over time throughout the help-seeking process. Semi-structured interviews were conducted with seven men aged 21–67 who had experienced depression and engaged with multiple forms of support. Data were analysed using reflexive thematic analysis, generating six themes: (1) The Complexity and Unpredictability of Depression; (2) From Fixing to Facing: Shifting Expectations of Help; (3) Opening the Door to Help-Seeking; (4) Barriers to Help-Seeking; (5) Experiences of Treatment; and (6) Re-imagining Support: Reducing Barriers and Improving Engagement. The findings highlighted the evolution of men’s expectations throughout help-seeking and how different forms of expectation–reality mismatch shaped future engagement. Lack of mental health literacy, stigma, and structural barriers remained problematic, whereas participants identified general practitioners as influential gatekeepers shaping early experiences of support. Within treatment, men demonstrated diverse preferences and emphasised the importance of tailored, responsive support alongside relational fit with practitioners. Men’s engagement with mental health services appeared dynamic, shaped by ongoing interactions between perceptions, expectations, and satisfaction across different stages of help-seeking. The findings suggested that improving mental health literacy, relational responsiveness, personalised care, and service accessibility may be more important than focusing solely on masculine norms. This may inform services that cater for individual differences among men rather than assuming uniform preferences.

title: Families of young people who self-harm: A mixed-methods systematic review of their experiences and needs
description: Background: Self-harm in young people is a pressing public health issue, with family support playing a crucial role in the young person’s prognosis. Concurrently, the impact extends to families themselves, who must navigate caregiving responsibilities while also requiring support. Understanding these experiences is key to providing more effective assistance in their caregiving roles. Aim: This mixed-methods systematic review aims to investigate the experiences, barriers, and needs of families of young people who self-harm. Method: Following PRISMA guidelines, a search was conducted across PsycINFO, PubMed, Web of Science, and Scopus databases in July 2024 and April 2025. A total of 31 studies were included in the review, and quality was assessed using the Mixed Methods Appraisal Tool. A narrative synthesis was employed for the quantitative data, while qualitative data were analysed using thematic synthesis. Results: The quantitative findings revealed two themes: (1) the repercussions of self-harm in the family, and (2) family support needs. Qualitative analysis identified four themes: (1) parents’ emotional and psychological impact; (2) impact on parenting and bond with young person; (3) disruption in wider family dynamics; and (4) barriers to and pathways for support. Conclusion: The review discusses the implications of these findings, offering recommendations for future research and improvements in family support services to alleviate caregiver burden and foster supportive environments for recovery.
authors: Beatriz Cação-Dias, Inês Areal Rothes, Cláudia Carmo, Cristina Nunes, Marta Brás

title: Differential item-functioning in the Eysenck neuroticism scale and its implications on the concurrent and predictive validity of the neuroticism construct
description:
Background. Gender differences in neuroticism are well known, with females consistently scoring higher than males across various scales, including the Eysenck Personality Questionnaire-Revised Neuroticism (EPQ-RN) scale. However, it remains unclear whether these differences are genuine or result from gender bias in psychometric instruments. Additionally, the implications of a potential bias for the predictive validity of neuroticism are not well understood. Given the role of neuroticism in predicting cognitive performance, this study aimed to investigate gender-based differential item functioning (DIF) in the EPQ-RN and its effects on the association between neuroticism and cognition.
Methods. Using data from 13,573 participants in the UK Biobank study, we conducted logistic regression DIF analyses on the EPQ-RN scale to identify gender-related item bias. Items were grouped into three constructs: bias-free, female-endorsed, and male-endorsed neuroticism. Cross-sectional linear regression and longitudinal structural equation modelling (SEM) were used to evaluate the association between neuroticism constructs and cognition, adjusting for age, gender, education, and depression.
Results. Significant DIF was identified in 10 of 12 EPQ-RN items, with six preferentially endorsed by females. However, females also scored higher on bias-free neuroticism, indicating actual gender differences. In cross-sectional analyses, bias-free and female neuroticism were significantly associated with poorer cognitive performance at baseline, whereas the effect of male neuroticism was conditional on the covariates in the model. Longitudinal SEMs did not reveal significant cross-lagged effects, suggesting neuroticism at baseline did not predict cognitive performance at follow-up, irrespective of the type of neuroticism subscale: bias-free, female, or male. 
Conclusion. Findings reveal substantial gender-based DIF in the EPQ-RN scale, likely overestimating neuroticism scores in females. Yet, even after removing biased items, females still exhibited higher bias-free neuroticism, supporting the notion of genuine gender differences. Importantly, the validity of the neuroticism construct as a predictor of cognition did not depend on DIF, with similar findings for bias-free and gender-specific scales.  These findings have implications for interpreting gender differences in cognitive aging, particularly when using widely adopted personality scales such as the EPQ-RN.
Keywords: Neuroticism, cognition, longitudinal, gender effects, gender bias, epidemiological research, lifespan development, psychometrics, differential item functioning.
authors: Dr Chris Patrick Pflanz, Sarah Bauermeister, John Gallacher

title: Developing LGBTQ+ Acceptance: Media as Resources for LGBTQ+ People and Close Others
description: Discrimination against LGBTQ+ people is increasing, and acceptance is declining. Prior research has focused on supporting LGBTQ+ individuals in developing their identity and self-acceptance, and on increasing LGBTQ+ acceptance among non-LGBTQ+ people. To examine media's roles in these processes, we studied how media serve as resources for LGBTQ+ self- and other acceptance. We conducted 31 semi-structured interviews with LGBTQ+ individuals and close others (e.g., friends, family). Through thematic analysis, we identified a pattern of resources that positioned media within a broader context. We also identified six motivations for media use that fulfill eight needs in developing LGBTQ+ acceptance. There was significant overlap in media use among LGBTQ+ and close others. We discuss implications, limitations, and future research directions.
authors: Joël Hendrix, Marieke Fransen, Gijsbert Bijlstra, Serena Daalmans
